mines.name: Lehigh Coal Mine (Mauch Chunk)
mines.description: The Lehigh Coal Mine (Mauch Chunk) was discovered by Army engineers probing the terrain for winter fuel to supply frontier posts, launching underground coal mining operations in 1820 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Lehigh Coal Mine (Mauch Chunk) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Mammoth Vein Mine (Pottsville)
mines.description: The Mammoth Vein Mine (Pottsville) was discovered by Army engineers probing the terrain for winter fuel to supply frontier posts, launching underground coal mining operations in 1840 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Mammoth Vein Mine (Pottsville) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Pottsville Coal Mines
mines.description: The Pottsville Coal Mines was staked by an experienced Pennsylvania miner who saw the familiar anticline structure from the ridge above, launching underground coal mining operations in 1830 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Pottsville Coal Mines had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: No. 9 Mine and Museum (Lansford)
mines.description: The No. 9 Mine and Museum (Lansford) was found when settlers digging a root cellar uncovered a thick seam of bituminous coal, launching underground coal mining operations in 1855 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the No. 9 Mine and Museum (Lansford) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Tamaqua Mine (PA)

A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Summit Hill Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Beaver Meadows Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

mines.name: Audenried Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Audenried Mine (PA) was staked after a homesteader noticed black rock outcropping along the creek bank and sent a sample to Denver, launching underground coal mining operations in 1862 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Audenried Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: McAdoo Mine (PA)
mines.description: The McAdoo Mine (PA) was found after a hard winter left settlers desperate for fuel and prompted intensive searching, launching underground coal mining operations in 1865 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the McAdoo Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: Delano Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Delano Mine (PA) came to light when a flash flood exposed a six-foot coal seam in the canyon wall, launching underground coal mining operations in 1870 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Delano Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Monte Cristo Mine (WA)
mines.description: The Monte Cristo Mine (WA) was located after a prospector noticed the blue-black tarnish of argentite in a canyon wall, launching silver mining operations in 1889 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Monte Cristo Mine (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest

mines.name: Rainier Mine (Monte Cristo)
mines.description: The Rainier Mine (Monte Cristo) was discovered by Mexican prospectors following a century-old Spanish trail to a recorded silver source, launching silver mining operations in 1894 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Rainier Mine (Monte Cristo) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest

Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. mines.name: Eureka Mine (Republic WA)

Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Eureka Mine (Republic WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Pride Mine (Republic WA)
mines.description: The Pride Mine (Republic WA) was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1898 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Pride Mine (Republic WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Reco Mine (Republic WA)
mines.description: The Reco Mine (Republic WA) came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Reco Mine (Republic WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Sterling Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest

By the time operations wound down, the Bohemia Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Myrtle Creek Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Eva Creek Mine (Fairbanks)

Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Eva Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Independence Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Independence Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1905 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Independence Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Montana Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Montana Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Montana Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. By the time operations wound down, the Yankee Creek Mine (Iditarod) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Arcadian Mine No. 2 (Keweenaw)

mines.name: Marenisco Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Marenisco Mine (Gogebic) emerged when a territorial geologist reported promising mineral indications in his annual survey, launching iron mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Minnesota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Marenisco Mine (Gogebic) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Colby Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Colby Mine (Gogebic) was located by methodical float sampling by a mining engineer working the district systematically, launching iron mining operations in 1888 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Minnesota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Colby Mine (Gogebic) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Montreal Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Montreal Mine (Gogebic) was discovered after local ranchers brought samples to a territorial assay office, launching iron mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Minnesota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Montreal Mine (Gogebic) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Newport Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Newport Mine (Gogebic) was located by methodical float sampling by a mining engineer working the district systematically, launching iron mining operations in 1888 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Minnesota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Newport Mine (Gogebic) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Gwinn Area Iron Mine
mines.description: The Gwinn Area Iron Mine was found when surface trenching exposed a rich vein of metallic ore, launching iron mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Wisconsin, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Gwinn Area Iron Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Lake Superior Iron Mine (Marquette)
mines.description: The Lake Superior Iron Mine (Marquette) was found when surface trenching exposed a rich vein of metallic ore, launching iron mining operations in 1872 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Wisconsin, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Lake Superior Iron Mine (Marquette) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

mines.name: Olyphant Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Olyphant Mine (PA) was found after a hard winter left settlers desperate for fuel and prompted intensive searching, launching underground coal mining operations in 1862 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Olyphant Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Taylor Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Taylor Mine (PA) came to attention when a farmer used local black rock for his forge and found it burned twice as long as wood, launching underground coal mining operations in 1862 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. By the time operations wound down, the Old Forge Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Dickson City Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Dickson City Mine (PA) was located by a Welsh miner who recognized the characteristic sheen and smell of quality coking coal, launching underground coal mining operations in 1870 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Dickson City Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: Mayfield Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Mayfield Mine (PA) was found after a hard winter left settlers desperate for fuel and prompted intensive searching, launching underground coal mining operations in 1870 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Mayfield Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Carbondale Mine No. 2 (PA)
mines.description: The Carbondale Mine No. 2 (PA) was found after a hard winter left settlers desperate for fuel and prompted intensive searching, launching underground coal mining operations in 1832 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Carbondale Mine No. 2 (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Azurite Mine (WA)
mines.description: The Azurite Mine (WA) emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Azurite Mine (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Harts Pass Mine (WA)
mines.description: The Harts Pass Mine (WA) was first staked by prospectors who traced a promising quartz vein up the mountain drainage, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1895 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Harts Pass Mine (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Ruby Creek Mine (WA)
mines.description: The Ruby Creek Mine (WA) was first staked by prospectors who traced a promising quartz vein up the mountain drainage, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1879 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Ruby Creek Mine (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - North Cascades National Park

Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Galice Mine No. 2 (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

mines.name: Squaw Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Squaw Creek Mine (OR) emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1854 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Squaw Creek Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Forest Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Forest Creek Mine (OR) came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1855 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Forest Creek Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest

mines.name: Bourne Mine No. 2 (OR)
mines.description: The Bourne Mine No. 2 (OR) was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1879 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Bourne Mine No. 2 (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

mines.name: Cracker Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Cracker Creek Mine (OR) emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1872 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the Cracker Creek Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.

mines.name: Unity Mine No. 2 (OR)
mines.description: The Unity Mine No. 2 (OR) was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1874 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Unity Mine No. 2 (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Ritter Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Ritter Mine (OR) emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1875 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Ritter Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

mines.name: Galena Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Galena Mine (OR) emerged from careful float sampling by a Denver assayer who spent two seasons tracing the vein uphill, launching silver mining operations in 1878 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Galena Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

mines.name: Cable Cove Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Cable Cove Mine (OR) was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1874 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Cable Cove Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Cornucopia Mine No. 2 (OR)
mines.description: The Cornucopia Mine No. 2 (OR) emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1890 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Cornucopia Mine No. 2 (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

By the time operations wound down, the Almeda Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Trail Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.

The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Tiller Mine (OR)

Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Tiller Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.

The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Canyonville Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

mines.name: Pewabic Iron Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Pewabic Iron Mine (Gogebic) was discovered after local ranchers brought samples to a territorial assay office, launching iron mining operations in 1886 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Minnesota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Pewabic Iron Mine (Gogebic) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Brotherton Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Brotherton Mine (Gogebic) was discovered after local ranchers brought samples to a territorial assay office, launching iron mining operations in 1887 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Minnesota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Brotherton Mine (Gogebic) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Yale Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Yale Mine (Gogebic) was located by methodical float sampling by a mining engineer working the district systematically, launching iron mining operations in 1886 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Minnesota, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Yale Mine (Gogebic) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Tecumseh Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Tecumseh Mine (Keweenaw) was identified by its brilliant blue-green gossan, visible from the valley floor, launching copper mining operations in 1859 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Tecumseh Mine (Keweenaw) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Crooked Creek Mine (Circle) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.

mines.name: Quartz Creek Mine (Fairbanks)

Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Quartz Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.

mines.name: Birch Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Birch Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. By the time operations wound down, the Birch Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Alder Creek Mine (Fairbanks)
mines.description: The Alder Creek Mine (Fairbanks) was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Alaska, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. By the time operations wound down, the Alder Creek Mine (Fairbanks) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.name: Cliff No. 2 Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Cliff No. 2 Mine (Keweenaw) was found when early settlers dug a well and struck a vein of solid native copper twelve feet down, launching copper mining operations in 1846 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Cliff No. 2 Mine (Keweenaw) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Eagle River Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Eagle River Mine (Keweenaw) came to notice after a territorial assay office received a sample of bright peacock ore from a local trapper, launching copper mining operations in 1843 amid the rush and optimism of the early frontier era. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. mines.name: Mandan Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Mandan Mine (Keweenaw) was found when early settlers dug a well and struck a vein of solid native copper twelve feet down, launching copper mining operations in 1857 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. By the time operations wound down, the Mandan Mine (Keweenaw) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Minnesota Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Minnesota Mine (Keweenaw) was staked after Native American guides showed early explorers copper stains on canyon walls, launching copper mining operations in 1856 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. By the time operations wound down, the Minnesota Mine (Keweenaw) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Quincy Mine No. 2 (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Quincy Mine No. 2 (Keweenaw) emerged after a geologist mapping for a railway company noted the copper oxide staining in a road cut, launching copper mining operations in 1856 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. By the time operations wound down, the Quincy Mine No. 2 (Keweenaw) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Calumet Conglomerate Shaft
mines.description: The Calumet Conglomerate Shaft was located by prospectors following old reports of 'green rocks' used by indigenous peoples as paint pigment, launching copper mining operations in 1870 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. By the time operations wound down, the Calumet Conglomerate Shaft had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Calumet and Hecla Shaft 2
mines.description: The Calumet and Hecla Shaft 2 was identified when a mining engineer recognized bornite and chalcopyrite in a creekside outcrop, launching copper mining operations in 1872 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Calumet and Hecla Shaft 2 had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Torch Lake Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Torch Lake Mine (Keweenaw) was located by prospectors following old reports of 'green rocks' used by indigenous peoples as paint pigment, launching copper mining operations in 1880 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. mines.name: South Range Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The South Range Mine (Keweenaw) came to notice after a territorial assay office received a sample of bright peacock ore from a local trapper, launching copper mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in Michigan, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. By the time operations wound down, the South Range Mine (Keweenaw) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Brownsville Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Brownsville Mine (PA) was identified when a geological survey noted thick carbonaceous shale capping the surrounding ridges, launching underground coal mining operations in 1858 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Brownsville Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Barnesville Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Barnesville Mine (PA) was found after a hard winter left settlers desperate for fuel and prompted intensive searching, launching underground coal mining operations in 1863 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. By the time operations wound down, the Barnesville Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

mines.name: Lattimer Mine (PA)
mines.description: The Lattimer Mine (PA) came to attention when a farmer used local black rock for his forge and found it burned twice as long as wood, launching underground coal mining operations in 1868 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in Pennsylvania, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Lattimer Mine (PA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.description: The Peshastin Creek Mine No. 2 (WA) was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1878 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. By the time operations wound down, the Peshastin Creek Mine No. 2 (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Tronsen Creek Mine (WA)
mines.description: The Tronsen Creek Mine (WA) was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1880 amid the rush and optimism of the Gilded Age. Located in Washington, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. By the time operations wound down, the Tronsen Creek Mine (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.

By the time operations wound down, the Mission Creek Mine (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.

The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. By the time operations wound down, the Winthrop Mine (WA) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Illinois River Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Illinois River Mine (OR) was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1852 amid the rush and optimism of the California Gold Rush era. Located in Oregon, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. By the time operations wound down, the Illinois River Mine (OR) had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
